title: Arnold Ventures Parole Project
text:
The Council of State Governments (CSG) Justice Center intends to illuminate parole release policies and practices in the United States and how they impact prison population sizes and lengths of stay, using data from the National Corrections Reporting Program (NCRP) from up to 45 states to:

Examine trends in their parole-eligible populations;
Conduct original empirical research on racial, ethnic, and gender disparities on the portion of sentences that are served in carceral settings past parole eligibility; and
Conduct deep dives into selected states to shed light on parole board decision-making. For these states, we will seek to identify any disparate decision-making and associated outcomes and recommend changes to policy and practice to prevent unnecessarily long prison stays while maintaining public safety.

title: Missing Data
text:
Questions

Depending on the state, can we assume that people in prison with missing parole eligibility data who have a sentence length of “Life, LWOP, Life plus additional years, Death” are not eligible for parole? Does offense type matter? For example, in the state of Georgia, 30% of people have missing parole eligibility data. Of these people, 70% had a violent offense and 20% had a Life/LWOP/Death sentence length. Of the people with a Life/LWOP/Death sentence length and missing parole eligibility data, 97% had a violent offense.

Depending on the state, can we assume that someone is not eligible for parole because they have a short sentence? Is time served so brief that the parole process might not be deemed necessary? For example, in Georgia, 83% of people with a sentence length of <1 year are missing parole eligibility information.

Do we need to take into account the prison sentencing system of the state to determine who is potentially eligible or not eligible for parole? For example, in Georgia, “While the grant of parole is discretionary, consideration for parole is often not; most inmates are entitled to automatic consideration for parole. In general, this occurs after serving 1/3 of the total sentence. However, at minimum, a person serving a misdemeanor sentence must serve 6 months and a person serving a felony sentence must serve 9 months. Inmates serving 21 years or more, but not a life sentence, become eligible after serving a 7 year term. In addition, inmates convicted of certain serious crimes (e.g. aggravated assault, incest, drug trafficking) are not eligible for parole until they have served 7 years or 1/3 of the prison term, whichever comes first.”
 Word document in Sharepoint for discussing missingness.

title: National Trends
text:
Although 16 states have abolished parole release – except for people whose crimes preceded abolition1 – most states rely on a paroling authority’s discretion to determine the actual timing of release for each person from prison, which then determines the overall prison population. The analysis shows that this discretion, cumulated across 34 states, accounts for at least 88,000 people currently in prison; over 35,000 people are in Texas alone. Nationally another 123,000 people will become eligible over the next one to five years. This analysis relies on support from Arnold Ventures, the NCRP and BJS data sources, and descriptions of parole release discretion in two series of publications from the Robina Institute.2

Percentage of PeopleNumber of People

























Note: NCRP Data is from 2020. The parole-eligible prison population only includes people with sentence lengths ranging between 1 to 24.99 years and who were incarcerated for a new court commitment (i.e., not including those who have been released and then revoked back to prison). States missing 100 percent of the NCRP data are depicted as “missing data” but many other states are missing a substantial portion of parole eligibility data.

Parole Eligibility Data by State (will remove)


The table provides a comprehensive overview of state-by-state data on the number of people in prison and their parole eligibility status, including the percentage of individuals currently eligible for parole, those who will be eligible in the next 1-5 years, and the proportion for which parole eligibility data is missing or low, due to the abolition of parole release or lack of data in certain states.

Total Population = total prison population, Total Prison Population (1 to 24.99 Year Sentences) = total prison population in prison for a new court commitment and sentence lengths between 1 to 24.99 years, currently eligible for parole/eligible for parole in the next 1-5 years/missing parole eligibility data = only for people in prison for a new court commitment and sentence lengths between 1 to 24.99 years.















Citations:

We refer to the pre-abolition parole eligible people as “legacy” cases. Some of the parole abolition states continue to reflect this legacy group in their data, e.g., Indiana with 655 people or 2 percent of the prison population; other abolition states likely have such a legacy group but do not reflect it in their data, e.g., Ohio.

Prison Admission Trends

Sentence about change in admissions from 2010 to 2020.







Admission Types

Prison admissions are shaped by two primary groups: new court commitments, or individuals newly incarcerated for a criminal offense, and parole returns and revocations, those who return to prison for failing to meet the conditions of their parole.

In Georgia in 2020, new court commitments accounted for 91% of prison admissions, while parole returns and revocations accounted for 9%.











Citations
Alper, Mariel E., et al. “Profiles in Parole Release and Revocation: Examining the Legal Framework in the United States.” Robina Institute of Criminal Law and Criminal Justice, 13 May 2022, https://robinainstitute.umn.edu/publications/profiles-parole-release-and-revocation-examining-legal-framework-united-states.

How is Parole Eligibility Determined?

In general, parole eligibility occurs after serving 1/3 of the total sentence, and after a minimum of  9 months. Inmates serving 21 years or more, but not a life sentence, become eligible after serving a 7 year term. In addition, inmates convicted of certain serious crimes (e.g. aggravated assault, incest, drug trafficking) are not eligible for parole until they have served 7 years or 1/3 of the prison term, whichever comes first.

United States. Bureau of Justice Statistics. National Corrections Reporting Program, 1991-2020: Selected Variables. Inter-university Consortium for Political and Social Research [distributor], 2022-11-28. https://doi.org/10.3886/ICPSR38492.v1.










How is Parole Eligibility Determined?

In general, parole eligibility occurs after serving 1/3 of the total sentence, and after a minimum of  9 months. Inmates serving 21 years or more, but not a life sentence, become eligible after serving a 7 year term. In addition, inmates convicted of certain serious crimes (e.g. aggravated assault, incest, drug trafficking) are not eligible for parole until they have served 7 years or 1/3 of the prison term, whichever comes first.







Prison Population Currently Eligible for Parole

Show breakdown of types:
                                         1) New court commitments/1-24.99 years
                                         2) New court commitments/<1 & >= 25 years 
                                         3) Parole returns/1-24.99 years
                                         4) Parole returns/<1 & >= 25 years








Focusing on New Court Commitments withSentences of 1 to 24.99 Years

For a nuanced analysis, this section narrows the focus to 
                                    individuals serving sentences of 1 to 24.99 years for new court commitments.

In 2020, there were 11,827 people who were in prison and currently eligible for parole. This group made up 46% of the prison population in prison for new court commitments with
                      sentences between 1 to 24.99 years.











Demographics





Race and EthnicityGenderAge







In 2020, among the prison population currently eligible for parole, Black, non-Hispanic individuals constituted the majority, representing 56 percent.
















In 2020, among the prison population currently eligible for parole, males constituted the majority, representing 93 percent.
















In 2020, among the prison population currently eligible for parole, people between the ages of 25-34 years constituted the majority, representing 39 percent.

















Criminal Offenses

In 2020, among the prison population currently eligible for parole, people with aggravated or simple assault offenses constituted the majority, representing 26 percent.














Sentence Lengths

In 2020, among the prison population currently eligible for parole, people with sentences between 10 to 24.9 years years constituted the majority, representing 66 percent.














Missing Data

Admission Types

Prison admissions are shaped by two primary groups: new court commitments, or individuals newly incarcerated for a criminal offense, and parole returns and revocations, those who return to prison for failing to meet the conditions of their parole.

In Georgia in 2020, new court commitments accounted for 91% of prison admissions, while parole returns and revocations accounted for 9%.

In 2020, Black, non-Hispanic individuals made up the largest portion of people in prison for both new court commitments and parole returns and revocations.















In 2020, male individuals made up the largest portion of people in prison for both new court commitments and parole returns and revocations.















In 2020, individuals aged 25-34 years made up the largest portion of people in prison for new court commitments, while individuals aged 35-44 years made up the largest portion of people in prison for parole returns and revocations.

















Criminal Offenses

Sentence about most common offense.













Sentence Lengths

Prison Admission Trends

Sentence about change in admissions from 2010 to 2020.







Admission Types

Prison admissions are shaped by two primary groups: new court commitments, or individuals newly incarcerated for a criminal offense, and parole returns and revocations, those who return to prison for failing to meet the conditions of their parole.

In Georgia in 2020, new court commitments accounted for 91% of prison admissions, while parole returns and revocations accounted for 9%.











Citations
Alper, Mariel E., et al. “Profiles in Parole Release and Revocation: Examining the Legal Framework in the United States.” Robina Institute of Criminal Law and Criminal Justice, 13 May 2022, https://robinainstitute.umn.edu/publications/profiles-parole-release-and-revocation-examining-legal-framework-united-states.

United States. Bureau of Justice Statistics. National Corrections Reporting Program, 1991-2020: Selected Variables. Inter-university Consortium for Political and Social Research [distributor], 2022-11-28. https://doi.org/10.3886/ICPSR38492.v1.










How is Parole Eligibility Determined?

In general, parole eligibility occurs after serving 1/3 of the total sentence, and after a minimum of  9 months. Inmates serving 21 years or more, but not a life sentence, become eligible after serving a 7 year term. In addition, inmates convicted of certain serious crimes (e.g. aggravated assault, incest, drug trafficking) are not eligible for parole until they have served 7 years or 1/3 of the prison term, whichever comes first.

                                         4) Parole returns/<1 & >= 25 years








Focusing on New Court Commitments withSentences of 1 to 24.99 Years

For a nuanced analysis, this section narrows the focus to 
                                    individuals serving sentences of 1 to 24.99 years for new court commitments.

In 2020, there were 11,827 people who were in prison and currently eligible for parole. This group made up 46% of the prison population in prison for new court commitments with
                      sentences between 1 to 24.99 years.











Demographics





Race and EthnicityGenderAge







In 2020, among the prison population currently eligible for parole, Black, non-Hispanic individuals constituted the majority, representing 56 percent.
















In 2020, among the prison population currently eligible for parole, males constituted the majority, representing 93 percent.
















In 2020, among the prison population currently eligible for parole, people between the ages of 25-34 years constituted the majority, representing 39 percent.

















Criminal Offenses

In 2020, among the prison population currently eligible for parole, people with aggravated or simple assault offenses constituted the majority, representing 26 percent.














Sentence Lengths

In 2020, among the prison population currently eligible for parole, people with sentences between 10 to 24.9 years years constituted the majority, representing 66 percent.

Prison Admission Trends

Sentence about change in admissions from 2010 to 2020.







Admission Types

Prison admissions are shaped by two primary groups: new court commitments, or individuals newly incarcerated for a criminal offense, and parole returns and revocations, those who return to prison for failing to meet the conditions of their parole.

In Georgia in 2020, new court commitments accounted for 91% of prison admissions, while parole returns and revocations accounted for 9%.











Citations
Alper, Mariel E., et al. “Profiles in Parole Release and Revocation: Examining the Legal Framework in the United States.” Robina Institute of Criminal Law and Criminal Justice, 13 May 2022, https://robinainstitute.umn.edu/publications/profiles-parole-release-and-revocation-examining-legal-framework-united-states.
Carson, Ann E. “Prisoners in 2020 – Statistical Tables.” Bureau of Justice Statistics, 1 Dec. 2020, bjs.ojp.gov/library/publications/prisoners-2020-statistical-tables.
Robina Institute of Criminal Law and Criminal Justice. “Prison-Release Discretion and Prison Population Size State Reports.” Accessed November 8, 2023. https://robinainstitute.umn.edu/publications/prison-release-discretion-and-prison-population-size-state-reports.
United States. Bureau of Justice Statistics. National Corrections Reporting Program, 1991-2020: Selected Variables. Inter-university Consortium for Political and Social Research [distributor], 2022-11-28. https://doi.org/10.3886/ICPSR38492.v1.










How is Parole Eligibility Determined?

In general, parole eligibility occurs after serving 1/3 of the total sentence, and after a minimum of  9 months. Inmates serving 21 years or more, but not a life sentence, become eligible after serving a 7 year term. In addition, inmates convicted of certain serious crimes (e.g. aggravated assault, incest, drug trafficking) are not eligible for parole until they have served 7 years or 1/3 of the prison term, whichever comes first.







Prison Population Currently Eligible for Parole

Show breakdown of types:
                                         1) New court commitments/1-24.99 years
                                         2) New court commitments/<1 & >= 25 years 
                                         3) Parole returns/1-24.99 years
                                         4) Parole returns/<1 & >= 25 years








Focusing on New Court Commitments withSentences of 1 to 24.99 Years

For a nuanced analysis, this section narrows the focus to 
                                    individuals serving sentences of 1 to 24.99 years for new court commitments.

In 2020, there were 11,827 people who were in prison and currently eligible for parole. This group made up 46% of the prison population in prison for new court commitments with
                      sentences between 1 to 24.99 years.











Demographics





Race and EthnicityGenderAge







In 2020, among the prison population currently eligible for parole, Black, non-Hispanic individuals constituted the majority, representing 56 percent.
















In 2020, among the prison population currently eligible for parole, males constituted the majority, representing 93 percent.
















In 2020, among the prison population currently eligible for parole, people between the ages of 25-34 years constituted the majority, representing 39 percent.

















Criminal Offenses

In 2020, among the prison population currently eligible for parole, people with aggravated or simple assault offenses constituted the majority, representing 26 percent.














Sentence Lengths

In 2020, among the prison population currently eligible for parole, people with sentences between 10 to 24.9 years years constituted the majority, representing 66 percent.














Missing Data

This section will have information about missing parole eligibility data for the state once new NCRP data is released.

In 2020, Black, non-Hispanic individuals made up the largest portion of people in prison for both new court commitments and parole returns and revocations.

Prison Admission Trends

Sentence about change in admissions from 2010 to 2020.







Admission Types

Prison admissions are shaped by two primary groups: new court commitments, or individuals newly incarcerated for a criminal offense, and parole returns and revocations, those who return to prison for failing to meet the conditions of their parole.

In Georgia in 2020, new court commitments accounted for 91% of prison admissions, while parole returns and revocations accounted for 9%.











Citations
Alper, Mariel E., et al. “Profiles in Parole Release and Revocation: Examining the Legal Framework in the United States.” Robina Institute of Criminal Law and Criminal Justice, 13 May 2022, https://robinainstitute.umn.edu/publications/profiles-parole-release-and-revocation-examining-legal-framework-united-states.
Carson, Ann E. “Prisoners in 2020 – Statistical Tables.” Bureau of Justice Statistics, 1 Dec. 2020, bjs.ojp.gov/library/publications/prisoners-2020-statistical-tables.
Robina Institute of Criminal Law and Criminal Justice. “Prison-Release Discretion and Prison Population Size State Reports.” Accessed November 8, 2023. https://robinainstitute.umn.edu/publications/prison-release-discretion-and-prison-population-size-state-reports.
United States. Bureau of Justice Statistics. National Corrections Reporting Program, 1991-2020: Selected Variables. Inter-university Consortium for Political and Social Research [distributor], 2022-11-28. https://doi.org/10.3886/ICPSR38492.v1.










How is Parole Eligibility Determined?

In general, parole eligibility occurs after serving 1/3 of the total sentence, and after a minimum of  9 months. Inmates serving 21 years or more, but not a life sentence, become eligible after serving a 7 year term. In addition, inmates convicted of certain serious crimes (e.g. aggravated assault, incest, drug trafficking) are not eligible for parole until they have served 7 years or 1/3 of the prison term, whichever comes first.







Prison Population Currently Eligible for Parole

Show breakdown of types:
                                         1) New court commitments/1-24.99 years
                                         2) New court commitments/<1 & >= 25 years 
                                         3) Parole returns/1-24.99 years
                                         4) Parole returns/<1 & >= 25 years








Focusing on New Court Commitments withSentences of 1 to 24.99 Years

For a nuanced analysis, this section narrows the focus to 
                                    individuals serving sentences of 1 to 24.99 years for new court commitments.

In 2020, there were 11,827 people who were in prison and currently eligible for parole. This group made up 46% of the prison population in prison for new court commitments with
                      sentences between 1 to 24.99 years.











Demographics





Race and EthnicityGenderAge







In 2020, among the prison population currently eligible for parole, Black, non-Hispanic individuals constituted the majority, representing 56 percent.
















In 2020, among the prison population currently eligible for parole, males constituted the majority, representing 93 percent.
















In 2020, among the prison population currently eligible for parole, people between the ages of 25-34 years constituted the majority, representing 39 percent.

















Criminal Offenses

In 2020, among the prison population currently eligible for parole, people with aggravated or simple assault offenses constituted the majority, representing 26 percent.














Sentence Lengths

In 2020, among the prison population currently eligible for parole, people with sentences between 10 to 24.9 years years constituted the majority, representing 66 percent.














Missing Data

This section will have information about missing parole eligibility data for the state once new NCRP data is released.

Sentence Lengths

In 2020, among the prison population currently eligible for parole, people with sentences between 10 to 24.9 years years constituted the majority, representing 66 percent.

Prison Admission Trends

Sentence about change in admissions from 2010 to 2020.







Admission Types

Prison admissions are shaped by two primary groups: new court commitments, or individuals newly incarcerated for a criminal offense, and parole returns and revocations, those who return to prison for failing to meet the conditions of their parole.

In Georgia in 2020, new court commitments accounted for 91% of prison admissions, while parole returns and revocations accounted for 9%.











Citations
Alper, Mariel E., et al. “Profiles in Parole Release and Revocation: Examining the Legal Framework in the United States.” Robina Institute of Criminal Law and Criminal Justice, 13 May 2022, https://robinainstitute.umn.edu/publications/profiles-parole-release-and-revocation-examining-legal-framework-united-states.

United States. Bureau of Justice Statistics. National Corrections Reporting Program, 1991-2020: Selected Variables. Inter-university Consortium for Political and Social Research [distributor], 2022-11-28. https://doi.org/10.3886/ICPSR38492.v1.










How is Parole Eligibility Determined?

In general, parole eligibility occurs after serving 1/3 of the total sentence, and after a minimum of  9 months. Inmates serving 21 years or more, but not a life sentence, become eligible after serving a 7 year term. In addition, inmates convicted of certain serious crimes (e.g. aggravated assault, incest, drug trafficking) are not eligible for parole until they have served 7 years or 1/3 of the prison term, whichever comes first.

How is Parole Eligibility Determined?

In general, parole eligibility occurs after serving 1/3 of the total sentence, and after a minimum of  9 months. Inmates serving 21 years or more, but not a life sentence, become eligible after serving a 7 year term. In addition, inmates convicted of certain serious crimes (e.g. aggravated assault, incest, drug trafficking) are not eligible for parole until they have served 7 years or 1/3 of the prison term, whichever comes first.

In 2020, among the prison population currently eligible for parole, males constituted the majority, representing 93 percent.
















In 2020, among the prison population currently eligible for parole, people between the ages of 25-34 years constituted the majority, representing 39 percent.

In 2020, among the prison population currently eligible for parole, people with aggravated or simple assault offenses constituted the majority, representing 26 percent.














Sentence Lengths

Race and EthinicityGenderAge







In 2020, Black, non-Hispanic individuals made up the largest portion of people in prison for both new court commitments and parole returns and revocations.















In 2020, male individuals made up the largest portion of people in prison for both new court commitments and parole returns and revocations.















In 2020, individuals aged 25-34 years made up the largest portion of people in prison for new court commitments, while individuals aged 35-44 years made up the largest portion of people in prison for parole returns and revocations.

Sentence about most common offense.













Sentence Lengths

United States. Bureau of Justice Statistics. National Corrections Reporting Program, 1991-2020: Selected Variables. Inter-university Consortium for Political and Social Research [distributor], 2022-11-28. https://doi.org/10.3886/ICPSR38492.v1.











Prison Admission Trends

Sentence about change in admissions from 2010 to 2020.







Admission Types

Prison admissions are shaped by two primary groups: new court commitments, or individuals newly incarcerated for a criminal offense, and parole returns and revocations, those who return to prison for failing to meet the conditions of their parole.

In Georgia in 2020, new court commitments accounted for 91% of prison admissions, while parole returns and revocations accounted for 9%.











Citations
Alper, Mariel E., et al. “Profiles in Parole Release and Revocation: Examining the Legal Framework in the United States.” Robina Institute of Criminal Law and Criminal Justice, 13 May 2022, https://robinainstitute.umn.edu/publications/profiles-parole-release-and-revocation-examining-legal-framework-united-states.

United States. Bureau of Justice Statistics. National Corrections Reporting Program, 1991-2020: Selected Variables. Inter-university Consortium for Political and Social Research [distributor], 2022-11-28. https://doi.org/10.3886/ICPSR38492.v1.










How is Parole Eligibility Determined?

In general, parole eligibility occurs after serving 1/3 of the total sentence, and after a minimum of  9 months. Inmates serving 21 years or more, but not a life sentence, become eligible after serving a 7 year term. In addition, inmates convicted of certain serious crimes (e.g. aggravated assault, incest, drug trafficking) are not eligible for parole until they have served 7 years or 1/3 of the prison term, whichever comes first.

                                         3) Parole returns/1-24.99 years
                                         4) Parole returns/<1 & >= 25 years








Focusing on New Court Commitments withSentences of 1 to 24.99 Years

For a nuanced analysis, this section narrows the focus to 
                                    individuals serving sentences of 1 to 24.99 years for new court commitments.

In 2020, there were 11,827 people who were in prison and currently eligible for parole. This group made up 46% of the prison population in prison for new court commitments with
                      sentences between 1 to 24.99 years.











Demographics





Race and EthnicityGenderAge







In 2020, among the prison population currently eligible for parole, Black, non-Hispanic individuals constituted the majority, representing 56 percent.
















In 2020, among the prison population currently eligible for parole, males constituted the majority, representing 93 percent.
















In 2020, among the prison population currently eligible for parole, people between the ages of 25-34 years constituted the majority, representing 39 percent.

















Criminal Offenses

In 2020, among the prison population currently eligible for parole, people with aggravated or simple assault offenses constituted the majority, representing 26 percent.














Sentence Lengths

In 2020, among the prison population currently eligible for parole, people with sentences between 10 to 24.9 years years constituted the majority, representing 66 percent.

Prison Admission Trends

Sentence about change in admissions from 2010 to 2020.







Admission Types

Prison admissions are shaped by two primary groups: new court commitments, or individuals newly incarcerated for a criminal offense, and parole returns and revocations, those who return to prison for failing to meet the conditions of their parole.

In Georgia in 2020, new court commitments accounted for 91% of prison admissions, while parole returns and revocations accounted for 9%.











Citations
Alper, Mariel E., et al. “Profiles in Parole Release and Revocation: Examining the Legal Framework in the United States.” Robina Institute of Criminal Law and Criminal Justice, 13 May 2022, https://robinainstitute.umn.edu/publications/profiles-parole-release-and-revocation-examining-legal-framework-united-states.

Robina Institute of Criminal Law and Criminal Justice. “Prison-Release Discretion and Prison Population Size State Reports.” Accessed November 8, 2023. https://robinainstitute.umn.edu/publications/prison-release-discretion-and-prison-population-size-state-reports.
United States. Bureau of Justice Statistics. National Corrections Reporting Program, 1991-2020: Selected Variables. Inter-university Consortium for Political and Social Research [distributor], 2022-11-28. https://doi.org/10.3886/ICPSR38492.v1.










How is Parole Eligibility Determined?

In general, parole eligibility occurs after serving 1/3 of the total sentence, and after a minimum of  9 months. Inmates serving 21 years or more, but not a life sentence, become eligible after serving a 7 year term. In addition, inmates convicted of certain serious crimes (e.g. aggravated assault, incest, drug trafficking) are not eligible for parole until they have served 7 years or 1/3 of the prison term, whichever comes first.

                                         3) Parole returns/1-24.99 years
                                         4) Parole returns/<1 & >= 25 years








Focusing on New Court Commitments withSentences of 1 to 24.99 Years

For a nuanced analysis, this section narrows the focus to 
                                    individuals serving sentences of 1 to 24.99 years for new court commitments.

In 2020, there were 11,827 people who were in prison and currently eligible for parole. This group made up 46% of the prison population in prison for new court commitments with
                      sentences between 1 to 24.99 years.











Demographics





Race and EthnicityGenderAge







In 2020, among the prison population currently eligible for parole, Black, non-Hispanic individuals constituted the majority, representing 56 percent.
















In 2020, among the prison population currently eligible for parole, males constituted the majority, representing 93 percent.
















In 2020, among the prison population currently eligible for parole, people between the ages of 25-34 years constituted the majority, representing 39 percent.

















Criminal Offenses

In 2020, among the prison population currently eligible for parole, people with aggravated or simple assault offenses constituted the majority, representing 26 percent.














Sentence Lengths

In 2020, among the prison population currently eligible for parole, people with sentences between 10 to 24.9 years years constituted the majority, representing 66 percent.














Missing Data

In 2020, Black, non-Hispanic individuals made up the largest portion of people in prison for both new court commitments and parole returns and revocations.

In 2020, individuals aged 25-34 years made up the largest portion of people in prison for new court commitments, while individuals aged 35-44 years made up the largest portion of people in prison for parole returns and revocations.

Prison Admission Trends

Sentence about change in admissions from 2010 to 2020.







Admission Types

Prison admissions are shaped by two primary groups: new court commitments, or individuals newly incarcerated for a criminal offense, and parole returns and revocations, those who return to prison for failing to meet the conditions of their parole.

In Georgia in 2020, new court commitments accounted for 91% of prison admissions, while parole returns and revocations accounted for 9%.











Citations
Alper, Mariel E., et al. “Profiles in Parole Release and Revocation: Examining the Legal Framework in the United States.” Robina Institute of Criminal Law and Criminal Justice, 13 May 2022, https://robinainstitute.umn.edu/publications/profiles-parole-release-and-revocation-examining-legal-framework-united-states.
Carson, Ann E. “Prisoners in 2020 – Statistical Tables.” Bureau of Justice Statistics, 1 Dec. 2020, bjs.ojp.gov/library/publications/prisoners-2020-statistical-tables.
Robina Institute of Criminal Law and Criminal Justice. “Prison-Release Discretion and Prison Population Size State Reports.” Accessed November 8, 2023. https://robinainstitute.umn.edu/publications/prison-release-discretion-and-prison-population-size-state-reports.
United States. Bureau of Justice Statistics. National Corrections Reporting Program, 1991-2020: Selected Variables. Inter-university Consortium for Political and Social Research [distributor], 2022-11-28. https://doi.org/10.3886/ICPSR38492.v1.










How is Parole Eligibility Determined?

In general, parole eligibility occurs after serving 1/3 of the total sentence, and after a minimum of  9 months. Inmates serving 21 years or more, but not a life sentence, become eligible after serving a 7 year term. In addition, inmates convicted of certain serious crimes (e.g. aggravated assault, incest, drug trafficking) are not eligible for parole until they have served 7 years or 1/3 of the prison term, whichever comes first.







Prison Population Currently Eligible for Parole

Show breakdown of types:
                                         1) New court commitments/1-24.99 years
                                         2) New court commitments/<1 & >= 25 years 
                                         3) Parole returns/1-24.99 years
                                         4) Parole returns/<1 & >= 25 years








Focusing on New Court Commitments withSentences of 1 to 24.99 Years

For a nuanced analysis, this section narrows the focus to 
                                    individuals serving sentences of 1 to 24.99 years for new court commitments.

In 2020, there were 11,827 people who were in prison and currently eligible for parole. This group made up 46% of the prison population in prison for new court commitments with
                      sentences between 1 to 24.99 years.











Demographics





Race and EthnicityGenderAge







In 2020, among the prison population currently eligible for parole, Black, non-Hispanic individuals constituted the majority, representing 56 percent.
















In 2020, among the prison population currently eligible for parole, males constituted the majority, representing 93 percent.
















In 2020, among the prison population currently eligible for parole, people between the ages of 25-34 years constituted the majority, representing 39 percent.

















Criminal Offenses

In 2020, among the prison population currently eligible for parole, people with aggravated or simple assault offenses constituted the majority, representing 26 percent.














Sentence Lengths

In 2020, among the prison population currently eligible for parole, people with sentences between 10 to 24.9 years years constituted the majority, representing 66 percent.














Missing Data

This section will have information about missing parole eligibility data for the state once new NCRP data is released.

Prison Admission Trends

Sentence about change in admissions from 2010 to 2020.







Admission Types

Prison admissions are shaped by two primary groups: new court commitments, or individuals newly incarcerated for a criminal offense, and parole returns and revocations, those who return to prison for failing to meet the conditions of their parole.

In Georgia in 2020, new court commitments accounted for 91% of prison admissions, while parole returns and revocations accounted for 9%.











Citations
Alper, Mariel E., et al. “Profiles in Parole Release and Revocation: Examining the Legal Framework in the United States.” Robina Institute of Criminal Law and Criminal Justice, 13 May 2022, https://robinainstitute.umn.edu/publications/profiles-parole-release-and-revocation-examining-legal-framework-united-states.

United States. Bureau of Justice Statistics. National Corrections Reporting Program, 1991-2020: Selected Variables. Inter-university Consortium for Political and Social Research [distributor], 2022-11-28. https://doi.org/10.3886/ICPSR38492.v1.










How is Parole Eligibility Determined?

In general, parole eligibility occurs after serving 1/3 of the total sentence, and after a minimum of  9 months. Inmates serving 21 years or more, but not a life sentence, become eligible after serving a 7 year term. In addition, inmates convicted of certain serious crimes (e.g. aggravated assault, incest, drug trafficking) are not eligible for parole until they have served 7 years or 1/3 of the prison term, whichever comes first.

United States. Bureau of Justice Statistics. National Corrections Reporting Program, 1991-2020: Selected Variables. Inter-university Consortium for Political and Social Research [distributor], 2022-11-28. https://doi.org/10.3886/ICPSR38492.v1.










How is Parole Eligibility Determined?

In general, parole eligibility occurs after serving 1/3 of the total sentence, and after a minimum of  9 months. Inmates serving 21 years or more, but not a life sentence, become eligible after serving a 7 year term. In addition, inmates convicted of certain serious crimes (e.g. aggravated assault, incest, drug trafficking) are not eligible for parole until they have served 7 years or 1/3 of the prison term, whichever comes first.







Prison Population Currently Eligible for Parole

Show breakdown of types:
                                         1) New court commitments/1-24.99 years
                                         2) New court commitments/<1 & >= 25 years 
                                         3) Parole returns/1-24.99 years
                                         4) Parole returns/<1 & >= 25 years








Focusing on New Court Commitments withSentences of 1 to 24.99 Years

For a nuanced analysis, this section narrows the focus to 
                                    individuals serving sentences of 1 to 24.99 years for new court commitments.

In 2020, there were 11,827 people who were in prison and currently eligible for parole. This group made up 46% of the prison population in prison for new court commitments with
                      sentences between 1 to 24.99 years.











Demographics





Race and EthnicityGenderAge







In 2020, among the prison population currently eligible for parole, Black, non-Hispanic individuals constituted the majority, representing 56 percent.
















In 2020, among the prison population currently eligible for parole, males constituted the majority, representing 93 percent.
















In 2020, among the prison population currently eligible for parole, people between the ages of 25-34 years constituted the majority, representing 39 percent.

















Criminal Offenses

In 2020, among the prison population currently eligible for parole, people with aggravated or simple assault offenses constituted the majority, representing 26 percent.














Sentence Lengths

In 2020, among the prison population currently eligible for parole, people with sentences between 10 to 24.9 years years constituted the majority, representing 66 percent.














Missing Data

Admission Types

Prison admissions are shaped by two primary groups: new court commitments, or individuals newly incarcerated for a criminal offense, and parole returns and revocations, those who return to prison for failing to meet the conditions of their parole.

In Georgia in 2020, new court commitments accounted for 91% of prison admissions, while parole returns and revocations accounted for 9%.

In 2020, Black, non-Hispanic individuals made up the largest portion of people in prison for both new court commitments and parole returns and revocations.















In 2020, male individuals made up the largest portion of people in prison for both new court commitments and parole returns and revocations.















In 2020, individuals aged 25-34 years made up the largest portion of people in prison for new court commitments, while individuals aged 35-44 years made up the largest portion of people in prison for parole returns and revocations.

















Criminal Offenses

Sentence about most common offense.













Sentence Lengths

Prison Admission Trends

Sentence about change in admissions from 2010 to 2020.







Admission Types

Prison admissions are shaped by two primary groups: new court commitments, or individuals newly incarcerated for a criminal offense, and parole returns and revocations, those who return to prison for failing to meet the conditions of their parole.

In Georgia in 2020, new court commitments accounted for 91% of prison admissions, while parole returns and revocations accounted for 9%.











Citations
Alper, Mariel E., et al. “Profiles in Parole Release and Revocation: Examining the Legal Framework in the United States.” Robina Institute of Criminal Law and Criminal Justice, 13 May 2022, https://robinainstitute.umn.edu/publications/profiles-parole-release-and-revocation-examining-legal-framework-united-states.

United States. Bureau of Justice Statistics. National Corrections Reporting Program, 1991-2020: Selected Variables. Inter-university Consortium for Political and Social Research [distributor], 2022-11-28. https://doi.org/10.3886/ICPSR38492.v1.










How is Parole Eligibility Determined?

In general, parole eligibility occurs after serving 1/3 of the total sentence, and after a minimum of  9 months. Inmates serving 21 years or more, but not a life sentence, become eligible after serving a 7 year term. In addition, inmates convicted of certain serious crimes (e.g. aggravated assault, incest, drug trafficking) are not eligible for parole until they have served 7 years or 1/3 of the prison term, whichever comes first.

                                         4) Parole returns/<1 & >= 25 years








Focusing on New Court Commitments withSentences of 1 to 24.99 Years

For a nuanced analysis, this section narrows the focus to 
                                    individuals serving sentences of 1 to 24.99 years for new court commitments.

In 2020, there were 11,827 people who were in prison and currently eligible for parole. This group made up 46% of the prison population in prison for new court commitments with
                      sentences between 1 to 24.99 years.











Demographics





Race and EthnicityGenderAge







In 2020, among the prison population currently eligible for parole, Black, non-Hispanic individuals constituted the majority, representing 56 percent.
















In 2020, among the prison population currently eligible for parole, males constituted the majority, representing 93 percent.
















In 2020, among the prison population currently eligible for parole, people between the ages of 25-34 years constituted the majority, representing 39 percent.

















Criminal Offenses

In 2020, among the prison population currently eligible for parole, people with aggravated or simple assault offenses constituted the majority, representing 26 percent.














Sentence Lengths

In 2020, among the prison population currently eligible for parole, people with sentences between 10 to 24.9 years years constituted the majority, representing 66 percent.

In 2020, male individuals made up the largest portion of people in prison for both new court commitments and parole returns and revocations.

Prison Admission Trends

Sentence about change in admissions from 2010 to 2020.







Admission Types

Prison admissions are shaped by two primary groups: new court commitments, or individuals newly incarcerated for a criminal offense, and parole returns and revocations, those who return to prison for failing to meet the conditions of their parole.

In Georgia in 2020, new court commitments accounted for 91% of prison admissions, while parole returns and revocations accounted for 9%.











Citations
Alper, Mariel E., et al. “Profiles in Parole Release and Revocation: Examining the Legal Framework in the United States.” Robina Institute of Criminal Law and Criminal Justice, 13 May 2022, https://robinainstitute.umn.edu/publications/profiles-parole-release-and-revocation-examining-legal-framework-united-states.
Carson, Ann E. “Prisoners in 2020 – Statistical Tables.” Bureau of Justice Statistics, 1 Dec. 2020, bjs.ojp.gov/library/publications/prisoners-2020-statistical-tables.
Robina Institute of Criminal Law and Criminal Justice. “Prison-Release Discretion and Prison Population Size State Reports.” Accessed November 8, 2023. https://robinainstitute.umn.edu/publications/prison-release-discretion-and-prison-population-size-state-reports.
United States. Bureau of Justice Statistics. National Corrections Reporting Program, 1991-2020: Selected Variables. Inter-university Consortium for Political and Social Research [distributor], 2022-11-28. https://doi.org/10.3886/ICPSR38492.v1.










How is Parole Eligibility Determined?

In general, parole eligibility occurs after serving 1/3 of the total sentence, and after a minimum of  9 months. Inmates serving 21 years or more, but not a life sentence, become eligible after serving a 7 year term. In addition, inmates convicted of certain serious crimes (e.g. aggravated assault, incest, drug trafficking) are not eligible for parole until they have served 7 years or 1/3 of the prison term, whichever comes first.







Prison Population Currently Eligible for Parole

Show breakdown of types:

                                         2) New court commitments/<1 & >= 25 years 
                                         3) Parole returns/1-24.99 years
                                         4) Parole returns/<1 & >= 25 years








Focusing on New Court Commitments withSentences of 1 to 24.99 Years

For a nuanced analysis, this section narrows the focus to 
                                    individuals serving sentences of 1 to 24.99 years for new court commitments.

In 2020, there were 11,827 people who were in prison and currently eligible for parole. This group made up 46% of the prison population in prison for new court commitments with
                      sentences between 1 to 24.99 years.











Demographics





Race and EthnicityGenderAge







In 2020, among the prison population currently eligible for parole, Black, non-Hispanic individuals constituted the majority, representing 56 percent.
















In 2020, among the prison population currently eligible for parole, males constituted the majority, representing 93 percent.
















In 2020, among the prison population currently eligible for parole, people between the ages of 25-34 years constituted the majority, representing 39 percent.

















Criminal Offenses

In 2020, among the prison population currently eligible for parole, people with aggravated or simple assault offenses constituted the majority, representing 26 percent.














Sentence Lengths

In 2020, among the prison population currently eligible for parole, people with sentences between 10 to 24.9 years years constituted the majority, representing 66 percent.














Missing Data

This section will have information about missing parole eligibility data for the state once new NCRP data is released.

Demographics





Race and EthinicityGenderAge







In 2020, Black, non-Hispanic individuals made up the largest portion of people in prison for both new court commitments and parole returns and revocations.















In 2020, male individuals made up the largest portion of people in prison for both new court commitments and parole returns and revocations.















In 2020, individuals aged 25-34 years made up the largest portion of people in prison for new court commitments, while individuals aged 35-44 years made up the largest portion of people in prison for parole returns and revocations.

Prison Admission Trends

Sentence about change in admissions from 2010 to 2020.







Admission Types

Prison admissions are shaped by two primary groups: new court commitments, or individuals newly incarcerated for a criminal offense, and parole returns and revocations, those who return to prison for failing to meet the conditions of their parole.

In Georgia in 2020, new court commitments accounted for 91% of prison admissions, while parole returns and revocations accounted for 9%.











Citations
Alper, Mariel E., et al. “Profiles in Parole Release and Revocation: Examining the Legal Framework in the United States.” Robina Institute of Criminal Law and Criminal Justice, 13 May 2022, https://robinainstitute.umn.edu/publications/profiles-parole-release-and-revocation-examining-legal-framework-united-states.
Carson, Ann E. “Prisoners in 2020 – Statistical Tables.” Bureau of Justice Statistics, 1 Dec. 2020, bjs.ojp.gov/library/publications/prisoners-2020-statistical-tables.
Robina Institute of Criminal Law and Criminal Justice. “Prison-Release Discretion and Prison Population Size State Reports.” Accessed November 8, 2023. https://robinainstitute.umn.edu/publications/prison-release-discretion-and-prison-population-size-state-reports.
United States. Bureau of Justice Statistics. National Corrections Reporting Program, 1991-2020: Selected Variables. Inter-university Consortium for Political and Social Research [distributor], 2022-11-28. https://doi.org/10.3886/ICPSR38492.v1.










How is Parole Eligibility Determined?

In general, parole eligibility occurs after serving 1/3 of the total sentence, and after a minimum of  9 months. Inmates serving 21 years or more, but not a life sentence, become eligible after serving a 7 year term. In addition, inmates convicted of certain serious crimes (e.g. aggravated assault, incest, drug trafficking) are not eligible for parole until they have served 7 years or 1/3 of the prison term, whichever comes first.







Prison Population Currently Eligible for Parole

Show breakdown of types:
                                         1) New court commitments/1-24.99 years
                                         2) New court commitments/<1 & >= 25 years 
                                         3) Parole returns/1-24.99 years
                                         4) Parole returns/<1 & >= 25 years








Focusing on New Court Commitments withSentences of 1 to 24.99 Years

For a nuanced analysis, this section narrows the focus to 
                                    individuals serving sentences of 1 to 24.99 years for new court commitments.

In 2020, there were 11,827 people who were in prison and currently eligible for parole. This group made up 46% of the prison population in prison for new court commitments with
                      sentences between 1 to 24.99 years.











Demographics





Race and EthnicityGenderAge







In 2020, among the prison population currently eligible for parole, Black, non-Hispanic individuals constituted the majority, representing 56 percent.
















In 2020, among the prison population currently eligible for parole, males constituted the majority, representing 93 percent.
















In 2020, among the prison population currently eligible for parole, people between the ages of 25-34 years constituted the majority, representing 39 percent.

















Criminal Offenses

In 2020, among the prison population currently eligible for parole, people with aggravated or simple assault offenses constituted the majority, representing 26 percent.














Sentence Lengths

In 2020, among the prison population currently eligible for parole, people with sentences between 10 to 24.9 years years constituted the majority, representing 66 percent.














Missing Data

This section will have information about missing parole eligibility data for the state once new NCRP data is released.

Prison Admission Trends

Sentence about change in admissions from 2010 to 2020.







Admission Types

Prison admissions are shaped by two primary groups: new court commitments, or individuals newly incarcerated for a criminal offense, and parole returns and revocations, those who return to prison for failing to meet the conditions of their parole.

In Georgia in 2020, new court commitments accounted for 91% of prison admissions, while parole returns and revocations accounted for 9%.











Citations
Alper, Mariel E., et al. “Profiles in Parole Release and Revocation: Examining the Legal Framework in the United States.” Robina Institute of Criminal Law and Criminal Justice, 13 May 2022, https://robinainstitute.umn.edu/publications/profiles-parole-release-and-revocation-examining-legal-framework-united-states.

United States. Bureau of Justice Statistics. National Corrections Reporting Program, 1991-2020: Selected Variables. Inter-university Consortium for Political and Social Research [distributor], 2022-11-28. https://doi.org/10.3886/ICPSR38492.v1.










How is Parole Eligibility Determined?

In general, parole eligibility occurs after serving 1/3 of the total sentence, and after a minimum of  9 months. Inmates serving 21 years or more, but not a life sentence, become eligible after serving a 7 year term. In addition, inmates convicted of certain serious crimes (e.g. aggravated assault, incest, drug trafficking) are not eligible for parole until they have served 7 years or 1/3 of the prison term, whichever comes first.

                                    individuals serving sentences of 1 to 24.99 years for new court commitments.

                      sentences between 1 to 24.99 years.

United States. Bureau of Justice Statistics. National Corrections Reporting Program, 1991-2020: Selected Variables. Inter-university Consortium for Political and Social Research [distributor], 2022-11-28. https://doi.org/10.3886/ICPSR38492.v1.










How is Parole Eligibility Determined?

In general, parole eligibility occurs after serving 1/3 of the total sentence, and after a minimum of  9 months. Inmates serving 21 years or more, but not a life sentence, become eligible after serving a 7 year term. In addition, inmates convicted of certain serious crimes (e.g. aggravated assault, incest, drug trafficking) are not eligible for parole until they have served 7 years or 1/3 of the prison term, whichever comes first.







Prison Population Currently Eligible for Parole

Show breakdown of types:
                                         1) New court commitments/1-24.99 years
                                         2) New court commitments/<1 & >= 25 years 
                                         3) Parole returns/1-24.99 years
                                         4) Parole returns/<1 & >= 25 years








Focusing on New Court Commitments withSentences of 1 to 24.99 Years

For a nuanced analysis, this section narrows the focus to 
                                    individuals serving sentences of 1 to 24.99 years for new court commitments.

In 2020, there were 11,827 people who were in prison and currently eligible for parole. This group made up 46% of the prison population in prison for new court commitments with
                      sentences between 1 to 24.99 years.











Demographics





Race and EthnicityGenderAge







In 2020, among the prison population currently eligible for parole, Black, non-Hispanic individuals constituted the majority, representing 56 percent.
















In 2020, among the prison population currently eligible for parole, males constituted the majority, representing 93 percent.
















In 2020, among the prison population currently eligible for parole, people between the ages of 25-34 years constituted the majority, representing 39 percent.

















Criminal Offenses

In 2020, among the prison population currently eligible for parole, people with aggravated or simple assault offenses constituted the majority, representing 26 percent.














Sentence Lengths

In 2020, among the prison population currently eligible for parole, people with sentences between 10 to 24.9 years years constituted the majority, representing 66 percent.














Missing Data

This section will have information about missing parole eligibility data for the state once new NCRP data is released.

Admission Types

Prison admissions are shaped by two primary groups: new court commitments, or individuals newly incarcerated for a criminal offense, and parole returns and revocations, those who return to prison for failing to meet the conditions of their parole.

In Georgia in 2020, new court commitments accounted for 91% of prison admissions, while parole returns and revocations accounted for 9%.

In 2020, Black, non-Hispanic individuals made up the largest portion of people in prison for both new court commitments and parole returns and revocations.















In 2020, male individuals made up the largest portion of people in prison for both new court commitments and parole returns and revocations.















In 2020, individuals aged 25-34 years made up the largest portion of people in prison for new court commitments, while individuals aged 35-44 years made up the largest portion of people in prison for parole returns and revocations.

















Criminal Offenses

Sentence about most common offense.













Sentence Lengths

Prison Admission Trends

Sentence about change in admissions from 2010 to 2020.







Admission Types

Prison admissions are shaped by two primary groups: new court commitments, or individuals newly incarcerated for a criminal offense, and parole returns and revocations, those who return to prison for failing to meet the conditions of their parole.

In Georgia in 2020, new court commitments accounted for 91% of prison admissions, while parole returns and revocations accounted for 9%.











Citations
Alper, Mariel E., et al. “Profiles in Parole Release and Revocation: Examining the Legal Framework in the United States.” Robina Institute of Criminal Law and Criminal Justice, 13 May 2022, https://robinainstitute.umn.edu/publications/profiles-parole-release-and-revocation-examining-legal-framework-united-states.

United States. Bureau of Justice Statistics. National Corrections Reporting Program, 1991-2020: Selected Variables. Inter-university Consortium for Political and Social Research [distributor], 2022-11-28. https://doi.org/10.3886/ICPSR38492.v1.










How is Parole Eligibility Determined?

In general, parole eligibility occurs after serving 1/3 of the total sentence, and after a minimum of  9 months. Inmates serving 21 years or more, but not a life sentence, become eligible after serving a 7 year term. In addition, inmates convicted of certain serious crimes (e.g. aggravated assault, incest, drug trafficking) are not eligible for parole until they have served 7 years or 1/3 of the prison term, whichever comes first.

                                         4) Parole returns/<1 & >= 25 years








Focusing on New Court Commitments withSentences of 1 to 24.99 Years

For a nuanced analysis, this section narrows the focus to 
                                    individuals serving sentences of 1 to 24.99 years for new court commitments.

In 2020, there were 11,827 people who were in prison and currently eligible for parole. This group made up 46% of the prison population in prison for new court commitments with
                      sentences between 1 to 24.99 years.











Demographics





Race and EthnicityGenderAge







In 2020, among the prison population currently eligible for parole, Black, non-Hispanic individuals constituted the majority, representing 56 percent.
















In 2020, among the prison population currently eligible for parole, males constituted the majority, representing 93 percent.
















In 2020, among the prison population currently eligible for parole, people between the ages of 25-34 years constituted the majority, representing 39 percent.

















Criminal Offenses

In 2020, among the prison population currently eligible for parole, people with aggravated or simple assault offenses constituted the majority, representing 26 percent.














Sentence Lengths

In 2020, among the prison population currently eligible for parole, people with sentences between 10 to 24.9 years years constituted the majority, representing 66 percent.

Notes
The Relative Rate Index (RRI) measures racial and ethnic disparities by comparing rates between groups, often using White individuals as the reference. The RRI provides insight into the degree of overrepresentation or underrepresentation, with an RRI greater than 1 indicating that a particular racial or ethnic group is disproportionately represented.
